title: Information Theory for Sports Analytics
description: Master information theory concepts through real sports analytics scenarios. Choose your analytical strategy—entropy analysis or predictive modeling—and see how your decisions lead to championship glory or defeat.

nodes.title: The Information Problem
nodes.content: You're the newly hired analytics director for a professional sports team. You have access to massive amounts of data—play-by-play records, player tracking coordinates, coaching decisions, and historical outcomes. However, you realize that not all information is equally valuable. This season, you must decide how to apply information theory to gain a competitive advantage: Will you focus on understanding the entropy and unpredictability of your opponents, or will you build a prediction system based on information content? Your strategic choice will determine whether you win or lose.

nodes.title: Choosing Your Information Strategy
nodes.content: Two proven paths exist for leveraging information theory in sports. Which approach will define your season?

choices.label: Build a prediction model using information content

nodes.title: Entropy and Play-Calling Unpredictability
nodes.content: You decide to focus on Shannon entropy—a measure of uncertainty and randomness in opponent decision-making. In sports analytics, entropy tells you how unpredictable a team's play-calling is. A coach with high entropy calls diverse plays that are hard to predict; one with low entropy is highly predictable. You analyze your top rival: their 3rd-and-long situations show high entropy (many different play types), but their red zone plays have low entropy (always run-heavy). Understanding this information distribution helps you know where to focus your defense and where to expect surprises.

nodes.title: Exploiting Information Asymmetry
nodes.content: You've identified a critical insight: opponents are predictable in high-pressure situations. How will you act on this information?

choices.label: Use low-entropy patterns against them in playoffs
Ignore the entropy data and rely on gut feelings

nodes.title: Championship Victory Through Information Theory
nodes.content: Your team's defense exploited the low entropy in your rival's red zone play-calling, intercepting three crucial passes in the playoffs. In the championship game, your entropy-analysis model correctly anticipated 78% of opponent plays. Meanwhile, your own offense maintained high entropy to keep the opposing defense guessing. You win the title, and your name becomes synonymous with information-driven sports strategy. The team owner credits your work with securing the franchise's first championship in 20 years.

nodes.title: Predictable Patterns Lead to Defeat
nodes.content: Without entropy analysis, your team failed to recognize that opponents maintained low entropy in critical moments. In the playoff game, the opposing coach called the same run play four times in the red zone, and your defense, unprepared, allowed three touchdowns. Your team's analytics approach was discredited. The ownership decides to hire a new director who understands modern statistical methods. You leave the sports industry questioning whether analytics can truly drive competitive advantage.

nodes.title: Information Content and Predictive Power
nodes.content: You take a different approach: building a prediction system using information content. In information theory, information content measures how much a single outcome reduces uncertainty about the future. You calculate mutual information—how much knowing one fact (score, quarter, field position) reduces uncertainty about the next play. You train a machine learning model to predict opponent plays based on high-information-content features: down-and-distance, field position, time remaining, and recent play history. Your model achieves 72% prediction accuracy on validation data.

nodes.title: Predictive Excellence Secures the Playoffs
nodes.content: Your information-based prediction model becomes a competitive weapon. Defensive coordinators use your pre-snap predictions to call perfect coverage, resulting in league-leading interceptions. Your team secures the #2 playoff seed. While not guaranteed a championship, your system proves that information-theoretic approaches deliver measurable results. You're invited to speak at the MIT Sports Analytics Conference and receive lucrative consulting offers from three rival teams.
